summary: Martin Fowler and Jez Humble wrote an open letter protesting Pearson's support for the SOPA/PIPA legislation, which they viewed as fundamentally flawed despite sympathizing with its copyright protection intent. They argued the bills lacked due process protections, proposed technically unsound approaches that would undermine internet security, and were crafted without adequate engineering input. The letter called on Pearson to reconsider its support and take responsibility as a technology education company. Over 100 prominent technical authors, including Joshua Bloch, James Gosling, Grady Booch, and Eric Evans, co-signed the letter in solidarity.
key_points: The legislation's provisions for blocking websites lack due process, risking punishment of innocent parties, especially smaller organizations with limited legal resources
The technical approach of manipulating DNS protocols undermines internet security and contradicts efforts to improve it, while being easily circumvented by determined actors
Congressional proceedings showed no engineering expertise and were heavily tilted toward bill supporters
As a technology education company, Pearson has a responsibility to ensure copyright protection efforts are balanced and technically sound
The letter gathered signatures from over 100 influential technical authors, demonstrating broad industry opposition
money_quotes: Even the most serious criminals need to be treated with due process, otherwise we risk innocent people facing punishment.
Not just do these approaches undermine internet security, they are also ineffective to moderately determined people who can work-around the blocking that's being done.
We don't expect members of congress to understand software engineering issues, but we do expect them to listen to those that do.

It is important for those that wish to protect copyrighted works to ensure they take actions that balance the need to protect intellectual property with fair procedures in sympathy with the way the internet works.
The contrast between Pearson and O'Reilly has been particularly stark.
key_insight: Copyright protection legislation must be crafted with engineering input and due process safeguards, otherwise it risks undermining the very internet infrastructure it seeks to protect.
